worth_it_explanation: Private Marketplace (PMP) and Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deals carry negotiated pricing and a committed buyer, so they should clear ahead of the open auction and at the price the publisher agreed to. A deal that has stalled, expired, or sits at a floor and priority that let the open market undercut it leaks the revenue the negotiation was meant to secure. This check reviews deal health and the floor-and-priority alignment a publisher already has in OpenX, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete deal the publisher can re-activate or reprice.
intro: PMP and PG deals route a negotiated buyer to a publisher's inventory at an agreed price, sitting above the open auction in priority so the committed demand is honored before the inventory falls back to the open market. This subcategory is about the health of those deals and how their floors and priorities sit against the open auction, rather than the open-market floor strategy itself. These checks confirm that active deals are spending as expected and that the deal floors and priorities are set so a deal neither undercuts the open market nor gets undercut by it.

audit_checks.title: Deal health is not monitored, so stalled or expired deals go unnoticed
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A deal that a buyer has stopped spending against, or one whose date range has lapsed, still occupies a negotiated commitment but returns nothing. Without a regular read of deal spend and status, a publisher cannot tell an active deal from a dormant one, so committed demand that has gone quiet keeps displacing open-market competition on the inventory it targets while delivering no revenue.

educate.base:
A Private Marketplace (PMP) or Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deal is an agreement between a publisher and a specific buyer that routes the buyer to defined inventory through a deal ID at a negotiated price. The deal ID is the thread that ties the agreement together. In a header-bidding setup, the bidder responds with the deal ID on the bid, the wrapper carries it to the ad server, and a line item keyed to that deal ID claims the impression at the agreed terms. Because the deal is negotiated, it carries a date range that follows the agreement rather than running indefinitely.

Deals do not always spend the way the negotiation assumed. A buyer can shift budget to another publisher, pause a campaign, or let a deal go dormant while the deal ID stays live on the publisher side. A date range can lapse without anyone re-checking it. When that happens, the deal still sits in the priority order above the open auction, so it can be considered first on the inventory it targets, yet it returns little or nothing because the buyer is not bidding. The inventory either falls through to the open market a step later than it should, or, where the deal is set to be preferred, it can hold back open-market demand that would have paid.

The audit question is whether deal health is read on a regular cadence rather than assumed. A publisher who reviews spend, impressions, and win rate per deal ID can see which deals are active, which have slowed, and which have stopped, and can retire or renegotiate the dormant ones. A publisher who set the deals up and has not looked since cannot tell the live deals from the dead ones.

A few signs point to deal health going unmonitored:
  • No regular report breaks out spend, impressions, and win rate by deal ID
  • Deal IDs remain active in OpenX with date ranges that have already lapsed
  • The publisher cannot say which deals are currently spending and which have gone quiet
You may want to confirm that deal health is reviewed on a set cadence rather than left to assumption. One approach is to build a per-deal-ID report of spend, impressions, and win rate, read it on a regular schedule, and retire or renegotiate any deal that has stalled or expired.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a sponsorship-style PMP or PG deal is often tied to a campaign flight, so it is expected to go quiet when the flight ends. You may want to track each deal's spend against its agreed date range so an expired flight is retired rather than left active above the open auction, where a dormant deal can keep displacing open-market demand on a premium placement.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, deals with endemic buyers can run seasonally and slow sharply between peak periods. You may want to read deal spend by season so a deal that has gone quiet outside its peak is recognized as dormant rather than counted as live demand, and so its inventory falls through to the open market when the buyer is not bidding.

educate.learn_more_note: OpenX's detailed reporting and deal-management documentation for this is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the active deal IDs against their agreed date ranges, and read spend, impressions, and win rate per deal to separate the deals that are spending from those that have stalled or expired.

openx_ui.label: OpenX UI
steps: Open the deals section and list the active Private Marketplace (PMP) and Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deals with their deal IDs, buyers, and date ranges.
For each deal, note whether its date range is still current or has already lapsed.
Record which deals the publisher believes are spending and which are uncertain, so the reporting read can confirm or correct that belief.

openx_reporting.label: OpenX reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by deal ID, with spend, impressions, and win rate over a recent window.
Mark each deal as spending, slowed, or stopped based on its recent activity rather than its setup status.
Note any deal that is active in the platform but returning little or no spend, and any deal whose date range has lapsed while it remains live.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per deal ID, the agreed date range and the recent spend, impressions, and win rate, then marks each deal as spending, slowed, or stopped. A setup where deal health is read on a regular cadence and dormant or expired deals are retired or renegotiated passes. A setup where deals run without a spend read, or where lapsed deal IDs remain active above the open auction, is a fail recorded with the affected deal IDs and their status.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each deal ID's date range and recent spend, impressions, and win rate above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs Private Marketplace (PMP) and Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deals without a regular read of deal health, so stalled or expired deals stay active above the open auction while returning little or no revenue. A dormant deal ID keeps its priority on the inventory it targets, which can delay the fall-through to the open market or hold back open-market demand that would have paid. {{voice_recommend}} building a per-deal-ID report of spend, impressions, and win rate, reading it on a set cadence, and retiring or renegotiating any deal that has gone quiet or whose date range has lapsed. After the cleanup, keep the cadence so deal status stays current rather than assumed.

audit_checks.title: Deal floors and priorities are misaligned with the open auction
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A deal is meant to clear at its negotiated price and ahead of the open auction, but that only holds when the deal floor and priority are set to honor the agreement. A deal floor set below open-market clearing prices lets the open auction undercut the deal, while a deal priority that always wins regardless of price can clear a negotiated impression below what the open market would have paid, so a misalignment either way moves money in the wrong direction.

educate.base:
A Private Marketplace (PMP) or Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deal carries a negotiated price, and the publisher's job is to set the deal's floor and priority so that price is honored against the open auction. Priority decides the order in which a deal is considered relative to open-market demand. In a header-bidding setup, deal demand is generally meant to rank above the open auction so the committed buyer is honored, while a 'prefer deals' setting can be used to favor a deal even when an open-market bid is higher. The deal floor is the price the deal must meet, which should reflect the negotiated terms rather than the open-market floor.

The alignment can go wrong in two directions. If the deal floor sits below the prices the open market is already clearing on that inventory, the open auction can outbid the deal and the negotiated buyer loses impressions the agreement was meant to guarantee. Pulling the other way, if a deal is set to be preferred and clears regardless of price, a negotiated impression can be filled below what an open-market bid would have paid, so the preference that was meant to protect the deal instead leaves money behind. The goal is a deal floor and priority that honor the negotiated price without overriding stronger paid demand the deal terms do not require be displaced.

OpenX runs Prebid-compatible header bidding, so the deal mechanics follow the wrapper's deal handling. The bidder returns the deal ID and price, the wrapper carries the deal through, and the ad server's priority order decides where the deal sits against the open auction. The audit question is whether the deal floors and priorities reflect the negotiated terms and sit sensibly against the open-market clearing prices, rather than being left at defaults that let the two undercut each other.

A few signs point to deal floors and priorities being misaligned:
  • Deal floors sit below the average open-market clearing price on the same inventory, so the open auction can outbid the deal
  • A 'prefer deals' setting clears deals regardless of price while open-market bids on that inventory run higher
  • The publisher cannot state, per deal, why its floor and priority sit where they do relative to the open auction
You may want to confirm that each deal's floor and priority honor the negotiated price and sit sensibly against the open-market clearing prices. One approach is to compare each deal's floor and win behavior against the average open-market price on the same inventory, then adjust the floor or priority where the two undercut each other.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a premium PMP deal on a homepage or section-front placement should hold a floor that reflects the negotiated rate rather than the open-market floor, which is usually lower. You may want to check that the deal floor on premium inventory sits at the agreed price so the open auction does not undercut the deal on the placements the buyer is paying a premium to reach.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, deals with endemic buyers on high-intent product and category pages can carry rates above the open-market clearing price for that inventory. You may want to confirm the deal floor reflects that negotiated rate, and that a 'prefer deals' setting is not clearing the deal below an open-market bid that the higher commercial intent would have driven up.

learn_more.source_title: Prebid Server | Features | Deals for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: OpenX's detailed configuration documentation for deal floors and priorities is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review each deal's floor and priority against the open-market clearing prices on the same inventory to see whether the two honor the negotiated terms or undercut each other.

openx_ui.label: OpenX UI
steps: Open the deals section and record each active deal's floor, priority, and the inventory it targets.
Note whether a 'prefer deals' or equivalent setting is applied that clears the deal regardless of an open-market bid's price.
Record the inventory each deal covers so its floor can be compared against the open-market clearing price on the same inventory.

openx_reporting.label: OpenX reporting
steps: Build a report of average open-market clearing price (eCPM) on the inventory each deal targets.
Compare each deal's floor against that open-market clearing price, and note any deal whose floor sits below it, which lets the open auction undercut the deal.
For any preferred deal, compare its clearing price against the open-market bids it displaced, and note any case where the deal cleared below a higher open-market price.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per deal, the deal floor, the priority or 'prefer deals' setting, and the average open-market clearing price on the same inventory. A setup where deal floors reflect the negotiated price and sit at or above the open-market clearing price, and where preferred deals are not clearing below higher open-market bids, passes. A setup where a deal floor sits below the open-market price so the open auction can undercut the deal, or where a preferred deal clears below a higher open-market bid, is a fail recorded with the deals and the direction of the misalignment.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each deal's floor, priority, and the open-market clearing price on the same inventory above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more deals on {{site_name}} carry floors and priorities that sit out of alignment with the open auction. A deal floor below the open-market clearing price lets the open auction undercut the negotiated buyer, while a preferred deal that clears regardless of price can fill an impression below what an open-market bid would have paid, so the misalignment moves money in the wrong direction either way. {{voice_recommend}} comparing each deal's floor against the average open-market clearing price on the same inventory, confirming the floor reflects the negotiated rate, and adjusting the floor or the priority where the deal and the open auction undercut each other. After the change, recheck deal win rate and clearing price against the open market to confirm the negotiated terms are honored.
